knowledge.statement: Performance optimisation should be driven by profiling, not intuition. Measure first to find the actual bottleneck; optimising the wrong path wastes time and often introduces bugs.
Premature optimisation is the root of much evil (Knuth). Write clear, correct code first. Optimise only when a measurable performance problem exists and profiling has identified the cause.

Amdahl's Law states that the maximum speedup of a program is limited by the fraction that cannot be parallelised. If 5% of a task is serial, the theoretical maximum speedup is 20x regardless of how many cores are added.

The memory hierarchy (registers → L1 cache → L2 cache → L3 cache → RAM → SSD → HDD) has orders-of-magnitude differences in latency. Algorithms and data structures that maximise cache locality are consistently faster than theoretically optimal but cache-hostile designs.
Caching stores the result of an expensive computation so it can be returned quickly on subsequent requests. The fundamental trade-off is memory consumption and the risk of returning stale data.

Cache eviction policies determine which items are removed when the cache is full. LRU (Least Recently Used) is the most common. LFU (Least Frequently Used) is better for skewed access patterns. Choose based on actual access distribution.

Cache-aside (lazy loading) populates the cache only on a cache miss. The application reads from the cache, and on a miss, reads from the DB and writes to the cache. It avoids loading unused data but risks a thundering herd on cold start.

Write-through caching writes to both the cache and the DB on every write, keeping them consistent. Write-behind (write-back) writes to the cache immediately and to the DB asynchronously, improving write throughput at the cost of potential data loss.

Cache stampede (thundering herd) occurs when a popular cached item expires and many concurrent requests all miss the cache simultaneously, flooding the database. Mitigate with probabilistic early expiration, locking, or background refresh.

Redis is an in-memory data structure store used for caching, session storage, pub/sub messaging, and distributed locks. It supports strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, and bitmaps with optional persistence.

CDN (Content Delivery Network) caches static assets (JS, CSS, images) at edge nodes geographically close to users. It reduces latency for static content and offloads origin server bandwidth. Also useful for caching full API responses at the edge.

The N+1 query problem executes one query to fetch N parent records and then N additional queries to fetch related data. It is the most common database performance problem. Fix with JOIN, batch loading, or ORM eager loading.

Connection pool exhaustion occurs when all database connections are in use and new requests queue or fail. Every application instance should not open its own unbounded pool; use a shared pool (PgBouncer, HikariCP) and monitor pool saturation.

Indexing is the highest-leverage database performance tool. EXPLAIN ANALYZE identifies sequential scans on large tables and suggests index candidates. Adding the right index often improves query speed by 100x or more.

Query optimisation starts with EXPLAIN ANALYZE output. Look for sequential scans on large tables, nested loop joins on unindexed columns, and high row estimates vs actual row counts (stale statistics). Run ANALYZE to update planner statistics.

Pagination prevents returning unbounded result sets. Offset-based pagination (LIMIT/OFFSET) degrades as offset grows because the DB must scan and discard skipped rows. Cursor-based pagination (keyset pagination) is O(1) regardless of page depth.

SELECT * fetches all columns, including large text/blob columns the application may not need. Always SELECT only the columns required; this reduces network transfer, memory allocation, and prevents index-only scan suppression.

Batch operations (INSERT … VALUES (…),(…),…) are dramatically faster than row-by-row inserts. Batching reduces round trips, transaction overhead, and write-ahead log entries. Use batch inserts for bulk data loading.

Asynchronous I/O allows a thread to continue executing while waiting for a slow operation (network, disk). Async/await in Python (asyncio), Node.js (event loop), and Go (goroutines) enable high concurrency without proportional thread growth.

Thread pools limit concurrency to prevent resource exhaustion. A system under heavy load that spawns unlimited threads will exhaust memory and context-switch overhead. Size thread pools based on the expected concurrency and CPU count.

Back-pressure prevents a fast producer from overwhelming a slow consumer. Queues, semaphores, and rate limits implement back-pressure. Without it, buffers grow unbounded, memory is exhausted, and the system crashes rather than degrades gracefully.

Lazy loading defers expensive computation until the result is needed. It saves resources when a value is sometimes never needed. It adds complexity (null checks, first-access cost) and can cause unpredictable latency spikes.

Memoization caches the return value of a pure function keyed on its arguments. It is the function-level equivalent of caching and is effective for expensive computations called repeatedly with the same inputs.

Object allocation pressure is a common JVM and GC-managed language performance issue. Excessive short-lived object creation triggers frequent garbage collection pauses. Use object pooling, value types, or zero-copy techniques in hot paths.

HTTP Keep-Alive (persistent connections) reuses a TCP connection for multiple requests, eliminating the cost of TCP handshake and TLS negotiation for each request. It is enabled by default in HTTP/1.1 and mandatory in HTTP/2.
HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple requests over a single TCP connection, eliminating head-of-line blocking at the HTTP layer. HTTP/3 (QUIC) moves to UDP and eliminates TCP head-of-line blocking entirely.

Gzip and Brotli compression reduce HTTP response sizes by 60–90% for text-based content. Brotli achieves better compression ratios than gzip at comparable CPU cost. Enable at the reverse proxy layer for all text responses.

Rate limiting protects services from being overwhelmed by too many requests. Token bucket and sliding window are common algorithms. Rate limits should be per client (IP or API key), not global, to prevent one client from impacting all others.

Load testing establishes a performance baseline and finds the system's breaking point before production traffic does. Tools: k6, Locust, Gatling. Run load tests in an environment that mirrors production sizing.

Latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99, p999) reveal tail latency that averages hide. The p99 latency is what the slowest 1% of users experience. In microservices, a chain of 10 services each at p99 results in the 10th-percentile of the chain being slow.

Response time budgets assign fractions of the allowed end-to-end latency to each tier (DB: 20ms, downstream service: 30ms, processing: 10ms). Budgets make it clear where latency is being spent and prevent any single tier from consuming everything.

Profiling types: CPU profiling shows where processor time is spent; memory profiling shows allocations and leaks; flame graphs visualise CPU call stacks. Sampling profilers have low overhead and are safe in production.

Web Core Vitals (LCP, FID/INP, CLS) are Google's user-centric performance metrics for web pages. LCP measures load time of the largest visible element; INP measures input responsiveness; CLS measures layout shift. They directly affect SEO.

Critical rendering path optimisation minimises the time before the browser can render the first frame. Techniques: inline critical CSS, defer non-critical JS, preload key resources, minimise render-blocking resources.

Image optimisation is one of the highest-impact web performance improvements. Use WebP or AVIF formats, serve appropriately sized images (srcset), and compress images. Images are typically 60–70% of a page's byte weight.
Service workers cache assets and API responses in the browser, enabling offline capability and instant repeat visits. They are the foundation of Progressive Web Apps (PWAs).

Micro-benchmarking measures the performance of a small isolated piece of code. Results are frequently misleading due to JIT warmup, compiler optimisations, and measurement overhead. Always benchmark the full hot path in realistic conditions.

Performance regression testing compares benchmark results between versions and fails builds when performance degrades beyond a threshold. It prevents performance regressions from silently shipping to production.

Vertical scaling (bigger machine) is simpler than horizontal scaling (more machines) and should be tried first. It has a cost ceiling but avoids distributed system complexity. Many applications run perfectly fine on a single beefy server.

Stateless services can be scaled horizontally without coordination. Statefulness (sessions, local caches) prevents horizontal scaling unless state is externalised (Redis, DB). Design services to be stateless from the start.

Queue-based load levelling absorbs traffic spikes by placing requests in a queue and processing them at a sustainable rate. It trades latency for throughput stability and prevents cascading overload during sudden bursts.

Event loop blocking is the most common Node.js performance issue. CPU-intensive synchronous operations block the event loop and prevent all other requests from being processed. Move CPU work to worker threads or a dedicated service.

String concatenation in a loop is O(n²) in languages without immutable string interning. Use StringBuilder (Java), list + join (Python), or template literals (JS). This is a frequent cause of unexpectedly slow loops over large datasets.

Data serialisation performance matters in high-throughput services. Protocol Buffers (protobuf) and MessagePack are 2–10x faster to parse and 2–5x smaller than JSON. Use binary formats for internal service communication at scale.

Connection reuse for external HTTP clients avoids TCP and TLS setup costs. Initialise HTTP clients as singletons or module-level objects, not per-request. Per-request clients are one of the most common production performance mistakes.

Efficient sorting: Python's Timsort, Java's Arrays.sort, and most modern sort implementations are O(n log n) comparison sorts. For small integers or bounded-range data, counting sort and radix sort achieve O(n). Know when linear-time sorting applies.

Bloom filters are probabilistic data structures that test set membership with zero false negatives and configurable false positive rates, using a tiny fraction of the memory of a hash set. Used in databases and caches to avoid expensive lookups for non-existent keys.

Chunked streaming responses allow the server to start sending data before the full response is computed. They reduce time-to-first-byte (TTFB) for large payloads and are essential for LLM token streaming and large file transfers.

Time complexity characterises how runtime grows with input size. O(1) is constant; O(log n) is logarithmic; O(n) is linear; O(n log n) is linearithmic; O(n²) is quadratic. Nested loops over large datasets are the most common algorithmic performance trap.
